hints.junior: If your table feels slow as rows are added, check the listener count. One listener on the parent handles every row: present, future, and dynamically added.
hints.mid: event.target.closest() is the key: it walks up from the clicked element and finds the row or button you care about, even if the click landed on a nested span.
hints.staff: In vanilla JS: delegation + data attributes (data-action, data-id) on elements is the clean pattern. Avoid checking className or tagName; data attributes are more explicit.

hints.junior: Try adding 200 rows in broken mode. See the slowdown? Now try fixed mode. Same rows, one listener, instant.
hints.mid: Check the Event Listeners panel in DevTools Elements tab. Broken: dozens of entries on rows. Fixed: one entry on the container.
hints.staff: The memory footprint difference is significant at 1000+ rows. Each DOM listener is a retained reference: per-row listeners on removed rows that weren't cleaned up are classic memory leaks.

hints.junior: closest() is the trick: it walks up from whatever you actually clicked and finds the ancestor you care about, so one listener on the parent can catch clicks from a button, a span inside it, or an icon inside that.
hints.mid: event.target is what got clicked; event.currentTarget is where your listener lives. The bug almost everyone hits once is checking target.tagName instead of target.closest(), which breaks the moment there's a nested element inside your button.
hints.staff: Delegation quietly fails in three places: focus/blur don't bubble (use focusin/focusout), a child's stopPropagation() blocks your parent listener entirely, and events don't cross shadow DOM boundaries by default — none of these throw an error, they just silently stop firing.

hints.junior: You almost always want event.target.closest(selector), not event.target directly — a click on a nested span or icon still needs to resolve up to the actual row or button.
hints.mid: If delegation mysteriously stops firing, check computed pointer-events on the parent before anything else — pointer-events: none silently blocks the parent from ever receiving the click in the first place.
hints.staff: In a virtualized list, per-row listeners get re-attached on every scroll because the rows themselves unmount. Delegate on the scroller container instead — that's the one element that's actually always mounted.

anecdotes.junior: I once profiled a dashboard that took 2 seconds to render 500 rows: 80% of the time was attaching click listeners. Delegation cut it to 50ms.
anecdotes.mid: React developers sometimes add addEventListener manually per list item, not knowing React already delegates at the root. It adds listeners on top of React's own system.
anecdotes.staff: Event delegation is also the pattern behind 'virtual list' click handling: rows are recycled DOM nodes, and a single delegated listener handles all of them.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're making large tables fast by using one event listener instead of one per row. It's called event delegation, and it's one of the most useful DOM patterns.
sections.mid: This case covers event delegation: how event bubbling makes it work, why closest() is the key primitive, and when not to use it.
sections.staff: We're covering the full delegation toolkit: closest, matches, focusin/focusout for non-bubbling events, and the React synthetic event system interaction.

sections.junior: The concept chapter is the principle: read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
sections.mid: Connect the principle to your component API. What prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
sections.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

sections.junior: Try adding 200 rows in broken mode. See the slowdown? Now try fixed mode. Same rows, one listener, instant.
sections.mid: Check the Event Listeners panel in DevTools Elements tab. Broken: dozens of entries on rows. Fixed: one entry on the container.
sections.staff: The memory footprint difference is significant at 1000+ rows. Each DOM listener is a retained reference: per-row listeners on removed rows that weren't cleaned up are classic memory leaks.
